Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## EXPRESSIVENESS AND APPROXIMATION PROPERTIES OF GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS

## **Floris Geerts**

Department of Computer Science, University of Antwerp, Belgium floris.geerts@uantwerpen.be

## **Juan L. Reutter**

School of Engineering, Pontificia Universidad Cat´olica de Chile, Chile & IMFD, Chile jreutter@ing.puc.cl

## ABSTRACT

Characterizing the separation power of graph neural networks (GNNs) provides an understanding of their limitations for graph learning tasks. Results regarding separation power are, however, usually geared at specific GNN architectures, and tools for understanding arbitrary GNN architectures are generally lacking. We provide an elegant way to easily obtain bounds on the separation power of GNNs in terms of the Weisfeiler-Leman (WL) tests, which have become the yardstick to measure the separation power of GNNs. The crux is to view GNNs as expressions in a procedural tensor language describing the computations in the layers of the GNNs. Then, by a simple analysis of the obtained expressions, in terms of the number of indexes and the nesting depth of summations, bounds on the separation power in terms of the WL-tests readily follow. We use tensor language to define Higher-Order Message-Passing Neural Networks (or _k_ -MPNNs), a natural extension of MPNNs. Furthermore, the tensor language point of view allows for the derivation of universality results for classes of GNNs in a natural way. Our approach provides a toolbox with which GNN architecture designers can analyze the separation power of their GNNs, without needing to know the intricacies of the WL-tests. We also provide insights in what is needed to boost the separation power of GNNs.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) (Merkwirth & Lengauer, 2005; Scarselli et al., 2009) cover many popular deep learning methods for graph learning tasks (see Hamilton (2020) for a recent overview). These methods typically compute vector embeddings of vertices or graphs by relying on the underlying adjacency information. Invariance (for graph embeddings) and equivariance (for vertex embeddings) of GNNs ensure that these methods are oblivious to the precise representation of the graphs.

**Separation power.** Our primary focus is on the separation power of GNN architectures, i.e., on their ability to separate vertices or graphs by means of the computed embeddings. It has become standard to characterize GNN architectures in terms of the separation power of graph algorithms such as color refinement (CR) and _k_ -dimensional Weisfeiler-Leman tests ( _k_ -WL), as initiated in Xu et al. (2019) and Morris et al. (2019). Unfortunately, understanding the separation power of any given GNN architecture requires complex proofs, geared at the specifics of the architecture. We provide a _tensor language-based technique_ to analyze the separation power of general GNNs.

**Tensor languages.** Matrix query languages (Brijder et al., 2019; Geerts et al., 2021b) are defined to assess the expressive power of linear algebra. Balcilar et al. (2021a) observe that, by casting various GNNs into the MATLANG (Brijder et al., 2019) matrix query language, one can use existing separation results (Geerts, 2021) to obtain upper bounds on the separation power of GNNs in terms of 1-WL and 2-WL. In this paper, we considerably extend this approach by defining, and studying, a new general-purpose tensor language specifically designed for modeling GNNs. As in Balcilar et al. (2021a), our focus on tensor languages allows us to obtain new insights about GNN architectures.

1

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

First, since tensor languages can only define invariant and equivariant graph functions, any GNN that can be cast in our tensor language inherits these desired properties. More importantly, the separation power of our tensor language is as closely related to CR and _k_ -WL as GNNs are. Loosely speaking, _if tensor language expressions use k_ + 1 _indices, then their separation power is bounded by k-_ WL _. Furthermore, if the maximum nesting of summations in the expression is t, then t rounds of k-_ WL _are needed to obtain an upper bound on the separation power._ A similar connection is obtained for CR and a fragment of tensor language that we call “guarded” tensor language.

We thus reduce problem of assessing the separation power of _any_ specific GNN architecture to the problem of specifying it in our tensor language, analyzing the number of indices used and counting their summation depth. This is usually much easier than dealing with intricacies of CR and _k_ -WL, as casting GNNs in our tensor language is often as simple as writing down their layer-based definition. We believe that this provides a nice toolbox for GNN designers to assess the separation power of their architecture. We use this toolbox to recover known results about the separation power of specific GNN architectures such as GINs (Xu et al., 2019), GCNs (Kipf & Welling, 2017), Folklore GNNs (Maron et al., 2019b), _k_ -GNNs (Morris et al., 2019), and several others. We also derive new results: we answer an open problem posed by Maron et al. (2019a) by showing that the separation power of Invariant Graph Networks ( _k_ -IGNs), introduced by Maron et al. (2019b), is bounded by ( _k −_ 1)-WL. In addition, we revisit the analysis by Balcilar et al. (2021b) of ChebNet (Defferrard et al., 2016), and show that CayleyNet (Levie et al., 2019) is bounded by 2-WL.

When writing down GNNs in our tensor language, the less indices needed, the stronger the bounds in terms of _k_ -WL we obtain. After all, ( _k −_ 1)-WL is known to be strictly less separating than _k_ -WL (Otto, 2017). Thus, it is important to minimize the number of indices used in tensor language expressions. We connect this number to the notion of _treewidth_ : expressions of treewidth _k_ can be translated into expressions using _k_ + 1 indices. This corresponds to optimizing expressions, as done in many areas in machine learning, by reordering the summations (a.k.a. variable elimination).

**Approximation and universality.** We also consider the ability of GNNs to approximate general invariant or equivariant graph functions. Once more, instead of focusing on specific architectures, we use our tensor languages to obtain general approximation results, which naturally translate to universality results for GNNs. We show: ( _k_ + 1)-index tensor language expressions suffice to approximate any (invariant/equivariant) graph function whose separating power is bounded by _k_ -WL, and we can further refine this by comparing the number of rounds in _k_ -WL with the summation depth of the expressions. These results provide a finer picture than the one obtained by Azizian & Lelarge (2021). Furthermore, focusing on “guarded” tensor expressions yields a similar universality result for CR, a result that, to our knowledge, was not known before. We also provide the link between approximation results for tensor expressions and GNNs, enabling us to transfer our insights into universality properties of GNNs. As an example, we show that _k_ -IGNs can approximate any graph function that is less separating than ( _k−_ 1)-WL. This case was left open in Azizian & Lelarge (2021).

In summary, we draw new and interesting connections between tensor languages, GNN architectures and classic graph algorithms. We provide a general recipe to bound the separation power of GNNs, optimize them, and understand their approximation power. We show the usefulness of our method by recovering several recent results, as well as new results, some of them left open in previous work.

**Related work.** Separation power has been studied for specific classes of GNNs (Morris et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019; Maron et al., 2019b; Chen et al., 2019; Morris et al., 2020; Azizian & Lelarge, 2021). A first general result concerns the bounds in terms of CR and 1-WL of Message-Passing Neural Networks (Gilmer et al., 2017; Morris et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019). Balcilar et al. (2021a) use the MATLANG matrix query language to obtain upper bounds on the separation power of various GNNs. MATLANG can only be used to obtain bounds up to 2-WL and is limited to matrices. Our tensor language is more general and flexible and allows for reasoning over the number of indices, treewidth, and summation depth of expressions. These are all crucial for our main results. The tensor language introduced resembles sum-MATLANG (Geerts et al., 2021b), but with the added ability to represent tensors. Neither separation power nor guarded fragments were considered in Geerts et al. (2021b). See Section A in the supplementary material for more details. For universality, Azizian & Lelarge (2021) is closest in spirit. Our approach provides an elegant way to recover and extend their results. Azizian & Lelarge (2021) describe how their work (and hence also ours) encompasses previous works (Keriven & Peyr´e, 2019; Maron et al., 2019c; Chen et al., 2019). Our results use connections between _k_ -WL and logics (Immerman & Lander, 1990; Cai et al., 1992), and CR and

## 3 SPECIFYING GNNS

Many GNNs use linear algebra computations on vectors, matrices or tensors, interleaved with the application of activation functions or MLPs. To understand the separation power of GNNs, we introduce a specification language, TL, for tensor language, that allows us to specify any algebraic computation in a procedural way by explicitly stating how each entry is to be computed. We gauge the separation power of GNNs by specifying them as TL expressions, and syntactically analyzing the components of such TL expressions. This technique gives rise to _Higher-Order Message-Passing Neural Networks_ (or _k_ -MPNNs), a natural extension of MPNNs (Gilmer et al., 2017). For simplicity, we present TL using summation aggregation only but arbitrary aggregation functions on multisets of real values can be used as well (Section C.5 in the supplementary material).

## 4 SEPARATION POWER OF TENSOR LANGUAGES

Our first main results concern the characterization of the separation power of tensor languages in terms of the color refinement and _k_ -dimensional Weisfeiler-Leman algorithms. We provide a finegrained characterization by taking the number of rounds of these algorithms into account. This will allow for measuring the separation power of classes of GNNs in terms of their number of layers.

## 4.2 MAIN RESULTS

We first provide a link between _k_ -WL and tensor language expressions using _k_ + 1 index variables: **Theorem 4.1.** _For each k ≥_ 1 _and any collection_ Ω _of functions, ρ_ 1�vwl _[(] k[∞][)]_ � = _ρ_ 1�TL _k_ +1(Ω)� _._

This theorem gives us new insights: if we wish to understand how a new GNN architecture compares against the _k_ -WL algorithms, all we need to do is to show that such an architecture can be represented in TL _k_ +1(Ω), i.e., is a _k_ -MPNN, an arguably much easier endeavor. As an example of how to use this result, it is well known that triangles can be detected by 2-WL but not by 1-WL. Thus, in order to design GNNs that can detect triangles, layer definitions in TL3 rather than TL2 should be used.
We can do much more, relating the rounds of _k_ -WL to the notion of summation depth of TL(Ω) expressions. We also present present similar results for functions computing graph embeddings.

We also consider “augmented” GNNs, which are combined with a preprocessing step in which higher-order graph information is computed. In the supplementary material (Section D.3) we show how TL encodes the preprocessing step, and how this leads to separation bounds in terms of _k_ -WL, where _k_ depends on the treewidth of the graph information used. ## 7 CONCLUSION

Connecting GNNs and tensor languages allows us to use our analysis of tensor languages to understand the separation and approximation power of GNNs. The number of indices and summation depth needed to represent the layers in GNNs determine their separation power in terms of color refinement and Weisfeiler-Leman tests. The framework of _k_ -MPNNs provides a handy toolbox to understand existing and new GNN architectures, and we demonstrate this by recovering several results about the power of GNNs presented recently in the literature, as well as proving new results.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: This paper displays a high level of academic rigor and intellectual maturity, offering a sophisticated theoretical framework that advances the field’s understanding of Graph Neural Network (GNN) expressivity. By abstracting GNN architectures into a procedural tensor language, the authors successfully shift the analytical burden from complex, architecture-specific proofs to a more systematic evaluation of index usage and summation depth. The clarity of the motivation—reducing the "black box" nature of GNN separation power into a accessible "toolbox" for designers—is compelling and fills a significant gap in the literature. However, the primary challenge for the authors will lie in managing the potential disparity between the abstract elegance of the tensor language and the practical, pedagogical accessibility of their proposed framework. While the theoretical derivations are clearly articulated, the paper must ensure that the transition from abstract tensor calculus to the concrete design of GNNs remains intuitive for architecture researchers without shifting into purely formal methods that might isolate the practitioner. Overall, the work is highly promising, providing a unified lens that elegantly reconciles known empirical results with foundational graph theoretical concepts.

comments.title: Terminology mismatch: Folklore k-WL vs. Oblivious (k+1)-WL
comments.quote: We use the “folklore” k-WL of Cai et al. (1992)... While equivalent to “oblivious” (k + 1)-WL (Grohe, 2021)... care is needed when comparing to our work.
comments.explanation: The paper relies on the correspondence between index counts in Tensor Language (TL) and WL-test iterations but does not explicitly settle the ambiguity between 'folklore' (tuple-based) and 'oblivious' (variable-based) definitions. The authors acknowledge that 'care is needed' but fail to provide a formal definition of the version used, which is critical for verifying the index-alignment theorems presented later.

comments.title: Ambiguity in Index-to-WL Power Correspondence

comments.explanation: The text defines k-MPNNs via TL_{k+1} and claims this corresponds to k-WL. This nomenclature is counter-intuitive relative to the standard correspondence where k indices usually map to k-WL. While likely an intentional design choice, the mapping between the order 'k', the index count 'k+1', and the separation power 'k-WL' should be explicitly justified or unified to avoid misleading the reader regarding the separation power of the model classes.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a novel and elegant framework for analyzing the separation power and universality of GNNs by casting them as expressions in a purpose-built tensor language. The approach is conceptually compelling, as it reduces complex architectural analysis to simple syntactic checks—counting indices and summation depth—to derive bounds in terms of the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy. The writing is clear and well-structured, with the abstract and introduction effectively motivating the problem and summarizing contributions. However, a significant concern is the accessibility and practicality of the tensor language for GNN designers: while the promise of a "toolbox" is appealing, the paper's reliance on abstract tensor constructs may still require considerable expertise to apply to novel architectures beyond the examples given. Additionally, the connection between the tensor language and existing GNN definitions (e.g., via message-passing schemes) could be made more explicit in the early sections to lower the barrier for readers less familiar with algebraic formalisms. The claims about recovering and extending prior results are impressive, but the paper would benefit from a clearer discussion of the limitations of the approach—for instance, whether the bounds obtained are always tight, or whether the tensor language can capture all GNN variants (e.g., those using attention or edge features). Overall, this is a high-quality theoretical contribution with strong clarity in exposition, though its impact may be limited by the steepness of the technical framework and the need for more concrete guidance on translating GNN layers into tensor expressions.

comments.title: Theorem 4.1 claims equality for any collection Ω of functions, which is likely too strong without qualification
comments.quote: Theorem 4.1. For each k ≥ 1 and any collection Ω of functions, ρ_1(vwl_k^{(∞)}) = ρ_1(TL_{k+1}(Ω)).
comments.explanation: The theorem asserts that the separation power of TL_{k+1}(Ω) exactly matches that of infinite‑round k‑WL, for *any* collection Ω of functions. The paper’s summary earlier notes that Ω typically contains activation functions or MLPs, but the theorem statement in the passage carries no restriction. If Ω were empty or contained only trivial functions (e.g., constant zero), it seems highly unlikely that TL_{k+1} could simulate the iterative refinement of k‑WL, which requires injective mappings from multisets of colors to new colors. Even with arithmetic and equality, constructing such mappings is non‑trivial and may require at least the identity function or a non‑linearity. Without further qualification, the claim as written would mislead readers into thinking that the result holds even for completely degenerate choices of Ω. The context does not resolve this; the passage contains no note that a mild condition on Ω (e.g., containing the identity and at least one non‑linear function) is necessary. This is likely an overstatement or an omission of an implicit assumption.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim that (k+1)-IGN's closure equals functions with k-WL separation
comments.quote: Furthermore, Theorem 5.1 implies that (k+1)-IGN_ℓ consists of functions f satisfying ρ_1(vwl_k^(∞)) ⊆ ρ_1(f) and ρ_0(gwl_k^(∞)) ⊆ ρ_0(f), a case left open in Azizian & Lelarge (2021).
comments.explanation: Theorem 5.1 states only an upper bound on the separation power of a t-layered (k+1)-IGN: it is bounded by t*k rounds of k-WL. This does not, by itself, imply that the class of all (k+1)-IGN_ℓ functions (presumably with arbitrary layers and continuous activations) has a separation power exactly matching that of k-WL (i.e., that it can separate every pair that k-WL separates). To conclude that the closure consists of all functions less separating than k-WL, one also needs a lower bound showing that (k+1)-IGNs can simulate k-WL (or at least that their separation power equals that of k-WL). The passage's phrasing suggests Theorem 5.1 alone suffices, which is logically insufficient. While the paper likely establishes such a lower bound elsewhere (e.g., in discussion of universality), the cited theorem is only half of the story, making the statement misleading in isolation.

comments.title: Inconsistency between theorem statement and passage summary regarding the number of indices

The passage explicitly says that MATLANG can only be used for bounds up to 2-WL and is limited to matrices. The authors claim their tensor language is more general. However, the summary indicates that for TL_k+1, the bound is k-WL. For 2-WL, the required number of indices would be 3 (k+1=3 when k=2). The passage states MATLANG is limited to matrices (2D arrays), which corresponds to expressions with at most 2 indices, giving a bound of 1-WL, but then says it can be used for bounds up to 2-WL. This would require 3 indices under the TL framework, but matrices only have 2. This discrepancy is not fully resolved by context in this passage alone, but the summary resolves it by noting 'k+1 indices' and 'bounded by k-WL', so a 2-index expression gives a bound of 1-WL, not 2-WL. Thus the claim that MATLANG can get up to 2-WL seems inconsistent with the authors' own bound unless MATLANG's matrix language in Balcilar et al. However, the paper later refines this by comparing number of rounds with summation depth. So this is a case where the introductory prose simplifies for readability, and the context of the full paper might resolve it, but as presented in this extracted passage, it is somewhat ambiguous. So this is not flagged as an issue — it's just an ambitious claim that the paper presumably justifies later.

comments.title: Theorem 4.1 claims equality for any collection Ω of functions, which is likely too strong without qualification
comments.quote: Theorem 4.1. For each k ≥ 1 and any collection Ω of functions, ρ_1(vwl_k^{(∞)}) = ρ_1(TL_{k+1}(Ω)).
comments.explanation: The theorem asserts that the separation power of TL_{k+1}(Ω) exactly matches that of infinite‐round k‑WL, for *any* collection Ω of functions.  The paper’s summary earlier notes that Ω typically contains activation functions or MLPs, but the theorem statement in the passage carries no restriction.  If Ω were empty or contained only trivial functions (e.g., constant zero), it seems highly unlikely that TL_{k+1} could simulate the iterative refinement of k‑WL, which requires injective mappings from multisets of colors to new colors.  Even with arithmetic and equality, constructing such mappings is non‑trivial and may require at least the identity function or a non‑linearity.  Without further qualification, the claim as written would mislead readers into thinking that the result holds even for completely degenerate choices of Ω.  The context does not resolve this; the passage contains no note that a mild condition on Ω (e.g., containing the identity and at least one non‑linear function) is necessary.  This is likely an overstatement or an omission of an implicit assumption.

comments.title: Ambiguous variable reuse in example expression
comments.explanation: In the example that rewrites the treewidth-1 expression, the variable x₁ is reused as the summation variable in the inner sum. Although variable shadowing is allowed by the language semantics (the bound x₁ inside the inner sum is distinct from the free x₁ in the outer scope), this reuse may confuse readers. A fresh variable name (e.g., y) would make the scoping unambiguous. The rewritten expression is mathematically correct, but the presentation is sloppy and could lead to misinterpretation.

comments.title: Potential OCR corruption of fragment notation
comments.explanation: The notation 'TL(3[2])' is inconsistent with the paper's defined notation for the k-index variable fragment, which is TL_k(Ω). The intended symbol is almost certainly 'TL_3(Ω)'. A similar issue occurs later with 'TL(2[2])'. Since the context makes the intended meaning clear, this is a minor artifact of OCR rather than an author error, but it could momentarily puzzle readers who recall the earlier definition.

comments.title: Incorrect citation for round-bound claim on treewidth-1 expression
comments.quote: Proposition 4.5 tells that the layer is bounded by wl_1^(3) (and in fact by cr^(3)) in separation power.
comments.explanation: The passage attributes a round bound (3 rounds of 1-WL or CR) to Proposition 4.5. However, from the paper summary, Proposition 4.5 states 'Expressions in TL(Ω) of treewidth k are equivalent to expressions in TL_{k+1}(Ω).' This proposition only concerns the number of index variables, not the number of rounds of WL or CR. A round bound would depend on the summation depth of the expression (e.g., three nested summations suggest t=3 if a theorem like 4.4 is invoked). The citation thus appears mismatched. It is possible that the full paper has a different numbering, but given the summary provided, this is an inconsistency that could confuse a reader trying to verify the claim.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim that (k+1)-IGN's closure equals functions with k-WL separation
comments.quote: Furthermore, Theorem 5.1 implies that (k+1)-IGN_ℓ consists of functions f satisfying ρ_1(vwl_k^(∞)) ⊆ ρ_1(f) and ρ_0(gwl_k^(∞)) ⊆ ρ_0(f), a case left open in Azizian & Lelarge (2021).
comments.explanation: Theorem 5.1 states only an upper bound on the separation power of a t-layered (k+1)-IGN: it is bounded by t*k rounds of k-WL. This does not, by itself, imply that the class of all (k+1)-IGN_ℓ functions (presumably with arbitrary layers and continuous activations) has a separation power exactly matching that of k-WL (i.e., that it can separate every pair that k-WL separates). To conclude that the closure consists of all functions less separating than k-WL, one also needs a lower bound showing that (k+1)-IGNs can simulate k-WL (or at least that their separation power equals that of k-WL). The passage's phrasing suggests Theorem 5.1 alone suffices, which is logically insufficient. While the paper likely establishes such a lower bound elsewhere (e.g., in discussion of universality), the cited theorem is only half of the story, making the statement misleading in isolation.

comments.title: Ambiguous 'in fact by cr^{(3)}' claim could mislead about relative strength
comments.quote: bounded by wl_1^(3) (and in fact by cr^(3)) in separation power
comments.explanation: Earlier in the paper it is stated that ρ_1(vwl_1^{(t)}) ⊂ ρ_1(cr^{(t)}), i.e., 1-WL is strictly more separating than CR. Thus being 'bounded by' CR^{(3)} is a weaker (less restrictive) condition than being bounded by vwl_1^{(3)}. The phrasing 'in fact by cr^{(3)}' suggests a stronger or more precise bound, which is the opposite of the usual reading. Moreover, the A^3 layer uses unguarded summation (sum over all vertices), so it is not a priori clear that it falls into the guarded fragment GTL that yields CR bounds. The paper would need to argue, likely via treewidth and variable elimination, that it can be rewritten in GTL, but this is not done here. The ambiguity could lead a careful reader to incorrectly infer that the expression is bounded by CR (a weaker bound) as an improvement, or to misunderstand the relationship between the two algorithms.

comments.title: Citation mismatch: Maron et al. (2019a) vs. (2019b)
comments.quote: answer an open problem posed by Maron et al. (2019a) by showing that the separation power of Invariant Graph Networks (k-IGNs), introduced by Maron et al. (2019b), is bounded by (k - 1)-WL.

Given that the discussion is explicitly about k-IGNs and their separation bounds, it is highly probable that the open problem was posed in (2019b) itself, making (2019a) a likely citation typo. This should be verified and corrected to avoid misleading readers about the provenance of the problem.

comments.title: Typo in variable name in invariant function definition
comments.quote: A function f : G_0 → F is called invariant if f(G) = f(σ ⋆G) for any permutation π .
comments.explanation: The sentence defines invariance using a permutation σ but concludes with 'for any permutation π'. This is a clear typographical mismatch. The context consistently uses σ for the permutation acting on vertices and graphs, so π is almost certainly a mistake. It does not affect the mathematical meaning but creates a minor inconsistency that could momentarily confuse a careful reader.

comments.title: Ambiguous adjacency notation and missing operator in edge predicate semantics
comments.quote: [[ E(x, y), ν ]]_G := if ν(x) ν(y) ∈ E_G then 1 else 0
comments.explanation: The condition uses juxtaposition to denote an edge. While common in theoretical computer science and algebraic graph theory as a shorthand, it omits explicit tuple/set notation (e.g., `(ν(x), ν(y)) ∈ E_G` or `{ν(x), ν(y)} ⊆ E_G`). Combined with the missing comma/set operator in the raw extraction, this introduces a minor formal syntax error. Adding explicit product symbol or parentheses would improve precision and clarity for a broader audience without changing the underlying meaning.

comments.title: Notation and OCR artifacts obscure k-WL update rule
comments.explanation: The formal definition of the multiset M for the k-WL update rule contains significant OCR artifacts and malformed notation. Specifically, 'atp_k+1(G, v u)' concatenates a variable and a parameter without clear syntax, and the nested multiset brackets '{{{' and '' are inconsistent. The authors' intent is to collect, for each vertex u, the atomic types of (k+1)-tuples formed by extending v and the previous colors of v with u substituted at each position. This matches the standard folklore k-WL algorithm, but as written, the notation ambiguously specifies how the (k+1)-tuple is constructed and how the multisets are nested. This makes it difficult to rigorously verify the update step or implement it without guessing the intended structure.

comments.title: Missing subscript in predicate semantics
comments.explanation: Initially, I noticed the notation `col_G(ν(x)) s ∈ R` and was concerned that `s` was intended as a separate condition or variable. Upon checking the surrounding context and the definition of vertex labels, it is clear that `s` should be a subscript, i.e., the `s`-th component of the color vector: `col_G(ν(x))_s`. This appears to be an OCR/formatting artifact or a minor typesetting omission. It does not break the logic, as the surrounding text consistently uses `s` to index feature columns, but it should be corrected for mathematical precision.

comments.title: Tautological definition for k-MPNNs
comments.quote: Hence, a GNN _is a k_ -MPNN if its corresponding functions are _k_ -MPNNs.
comments.explanation: This sentence feels circular right after the authors define $k$-MPNNs as 'the class of functions that can be represented in TL$_{k+1}$(Ω)'. The intended meaning is likely 'a GNN is a $k$-MPNN if its layer functions can be represented in TL$_{k+1}$(Ω)'. While the logical intent is clear from context, the phrasing as written is tautological and slightly confusing. Clarifying that the property transfers from the underlying mathematical functions to the named architecture would strengthen the prose.

comments.title: Missing equation for semantics lifting
comments.quote:
Furthermore, we lift interpretations to arbitrary expressions in TL(Ω), as follows:

where, _ν_ [ _x → v_ ] is the valuation _ν_ but which now maps the index _x_ to the vertex _v ∈ VG_
comments.explanation: The text introduces the lifting of semantic interpretations 'as follows:' but the subsequent line is blank before jumping to 'where, ν[x ↦ v] is...'. This appears to be a severe OCR extraction error or formatting gap where the recursive semantic rules (for operations like `·`, `+`, scalar multiplication, `f()`, and `∑_x`) should be. As it stands, a reader cannot see how the semantics extend from base predicates to complex TL expressions. While I recognize this as a likely PDF-to-text artifact rather than an authorial oversight, it creates a significant logical gap in the provided passage that hinders reproducibility of the formal definitions.

comments.title: Identical garbled notation for distinct equality predicates
comments.quote: In GTL(Ω) only equality predicates **1xi = xi** (constant 1) and **1xi = xi** (constant 0) are allowed
comments.explanation: The text repeats the exact same garbled string '1xi = xi' for both the constant 1 and constant 0 equality predicates. Given the TL grammar defines predicates as 1_{x op y}, this is almost certainly an OCR or typesetting error that should distinguish between two different equality/disequality forms (e.g., 1_{x_i = x_j} or 1_{x_i = x_i} vs 1_{x_i \neq x_i}). As written, it ambiguously restricts both to the same symbol, undermining the formal definition of the guarded fragment.

comments.title: Variable shadowing/reuse in Proposition 4.5 example
comments.quote: And indeed, it is equivalent to the expression θ˜(x1) =[]x2[E(x1,x2)]·x1[E(x2,x1)] in TL(2[2])
comments.explanation: The example demonstrates variable elimination (reordering summations) to reduce a treewidth-1 expression to two indices. However, the reduced expression sums over x_1 in the second term (interpreted as [∑_{x_1} E(x_2, x_1)]), while x_1 is already the free index of θ˜. Reusing a free variable as a bound variable inside a product incorrectly shadows it, which would mathematically destroy the function's dependence on x_1. The inner summation should bind a fresh variable (e.g., x_3) to correctly represent the degree of x_2. This is a technical error in the worked example.

comments.title: Repetitive typographical error
comments.quote: We can do much more, relating the rounds of k-WL to the notion of summation depth of TL(Ω) expressions. We also present present similar results for functions computing graph embeddings.
comments.explanation: The word 'present' is repeated consecutively. This is a minor proofreading oversight that does not affect the mathematical content but disrupts readability.

comments.title: Misclassification of graph embedding networks as 1-MPNNs
comments.quote: So they are 1-MPNNs. Hence, their separation power is bounded by gwl[(] 1 _[t]_[)][,][in][accordance][with][Theorem][4.4][.]
comments.explanation: The authors formally define k-MPNNs as functions mapping vertex tuples to embeddings ($f: \mathcal{G}_s \to \mathbb{R}^{[\ell]}$), with 1-MPNNs specifically handling vertex embeddings ($s=1$). The readout layer described aggregates over all vertices to produce graph embeddings ($s=0$). Calling the resulting architecture a '1-MPNN' strictly contradicts the paper's own definition. While this is a common informal shorthand in the GNN literature (referring to the underlying message-passing core), it creates an inconsistency with the rigorous definitions established in Section 3. A more precise phrasing like 'the underlying vertex embeddings are 1-MPNNs' or 'the architecture is based on 1-MPNNs' would be technically accurate.

comments.title: Unexplained reduction in round bound after adding readout summation
comments.quote: Since egin[(] _[t]_[)][can][be][represented][in] TL[(] 2 _[t]_[)][(Ω)][, the readout layer can be represented in][ TL][(] 2 _[t]_[+1][)] (Ω), using an extra summation. ... Hence, their separation power is bounded by gwl[(] 1 _[t]_[)...
comments.explanation: The authors correctly note that adding a graph-level readout summation increases the expression's summation depth from $t$ to $t+1$, placing it in $\text{TL}_2^{(t+1)}(\Omega)$. However, they immediately claim the separation power is bounded by $t$ rounds of 1-WL, rather than $t+1$. For $k>1$, the paper explicitly states there is no clean depth-to-rounds mapping. For $k=1$, while the mapping is better understood, increasing the nesting depth typically increases the number of WL rounds needed to simulate the computation. The authors do not justify why the extra summation does not increase the bound to $t+1$ rounds, which could mislead readers about the tightness of the bound or the behavior of graph-level TL expressions.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation for required WL rounds in Theorem 5.1
comments.quote: Theorem 5.1. For any k ≥ 1, the separation power of a t-layered ( k + 1)- IGN s is bounded by the separation power of tk rounds of k-WL.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'tk rounds' is typographically ambiguous. The subsequent explanation ('The additional rounds ( tk ) are needed because...') clarifies that the authors mean the product $t \times k$. However, without a multiplication symbol or standard mathematical spacing, a careful reader could initially interpret 'tk' as a concatenated variable name or a single parameter. Using explicit notation like $t \cdot k$ or 't times k' would prevent confusion and align with standard mathematical writing conventions.

comments.title: Subject-verb agreement error in claim about IGNs
comments.quote: We also show that the separation power of ( k + 1)-Invariant Graph Networks (( k + 1)-IGNs) (Maron et al., 2019b) are bounded by k-WL, albeit with an increase in the required rounds.
comments.explanation: The subject of the sentence is 'the separation power' (singular), but the verb used is 'are' (plural). It should read 'is bounded'. This is a minor grammatical error that does not affect the technical content but slightly detracts from readability.

comments.title: Missing mathematical statement in Theorem 4.4
comments.quote:
**Theorem 4.4.** _For all t ≥_ 0 _, k ≥_ 1 _and any collection_ Ω _of functions, we have that:_

 Intuitively, in (1) the increase in summation depth by one is incurred by the additional aggregation needed to collect all vertex labels computed by gwl[(] 1 _[t]_[)][.]
comments.explanation: The formal mathematical statement for Theorem 4.4 is completely absent. The text jumps from 'we have that:' directly to 'Intuitively, in (1)...', indicating that the corresponding equation or inequality block failed to extract during PDF processing or is missing from the manuscript. Without the explicit bounds relating \rho_0(TL), \rho_0(gwl_k^{(t)}), and summation depth, the precise claim of the theorem cannot be verified or utilized. This is a critical gap for a paper whose core contribution is establishing exact correspondences between syntactic TL properties and WL separation power.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation for guarded MPNN classes
comments.quote: We denote by MPNNs and MPNNs[(] _[t]_[)] the corresponding “guarded” classes of 1-MPNNs.[3]
comments.explanation: The authors previously defined \text{MPNNs}^{(t)} as 'Guarded class of 1-MPNNs' in the notation list and \text{TL}_{k+1}(\Omega) as k-MPNNs. Here, they reuse the symbol MPNNs (without the GTL or guarded prefix) to refer specifically to the 'guarded' classes. This creates potential confusion with the standard 1-MPNN definition used earlier. While context suggests they mean the guarded fragment, explicitly using a different symbol (e.g., \text{G-MPNNs}) or clarifying that they are redefining the shorthand would improve precision.

comments.title: Grammatical phrasing obscures logical flow
comments.quote: The connection between the number of index variables in expressions and _k_ -WL remains to hold.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'remains to hold' is grammatically awkward and likely a typo for 'remains valid' or 'continues to hold'. While easily decipherable in context, it momentarily disrupts the reading of a key transitional sentence summarizing prior results before introducing the summation depth discussion. A minor editorial correction is needed.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation for required WL rounds in Theorem 5.1
comments.quote: Theorem 5.1. For any k ≥ 1, the separation power of a t-layered ( k + 1)- IGN s is bounded by the separation power of tk rounds of k-WL.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'tk rounds' is typographically ambiguous. The subsequent explanation ('The additional rounds ( tk ) are needed because...') clarifies that the authors mean the product $t \times k$. However, without a multiplication symbol or standard mathematical spacing, a careful reader could initially interpret 'tk' as a concatenated variable name or a single parameter. Using explicit notation like $t \cdot k$ or 't times k' would prevent confusion and align with standard mathematical writing conventions.

comments.title: Subject-verb agreement error in claim about IGNs
comments.quote: We also show that the separation power of ( k + 1)-Invariant Graph Networks (( k + 1)-IGNs) (Maron et al., 2019b) are bounded by k-WL, albeit with an increase in the required rounds.
comments.explanation: The subject of the sentence is 'the separation power' (singular), but the verb used is 'are' (plural). It should read 'is bounded'. This is a minor grammatical error that does not affect the technical content but slightly detracts from readability.

comments.title: Missing mathematical statement in Theorem 4.4
comments.quote:
**Theorem 4.4.** _For all t ≥_ 0 _, k ≥_ 1 _and any collection_ Ω _of functions, we have that:_

 Intuitively, in (1) the increase in summation depth by one is incurred by the additional aggregation needed to collect all vertex labels computed by gwl[(] 1 _[t]_[)][.]
comments.explanation: The formal mathematical statement for Theorem 4.4 is completely absent. The text jumps from 'we have that:' directly to 'Intuitively, in (1)...', indicating that the corresponding equation or inequality block failed to extract during PDF processing or is missing from the manuscript. Without the explicit bounds relating \rho_0(TL), \rho_0(gwl_k^{(t)}), and summation depth, the precise claim of the theorem cannot be verified or utilized. This is a critical gap for a paper whose core contribution is establishing exact correspondences between syntactic TL properties and WL separation power.

comments.title: Ambiguous notation for guarded MPNN classes
comments.quote: We denote by MPNNs and MPNNs[(] _[t]_[)] the corresponding “guarded” classes of 1-MPNNs.[3]
comments.explanation: The authors previously defined \text{MPNNs}^{(t)} as 'Guarded class of 1-MPNNs' in the notation list and \text{TL}_{k+1}(\Omega) as k-MPNNs. Here, they reuse the symbol MPNNs (without the GTL or guarded prefix) to refer specifically to the 'guarded' classes. This creates potential confusion with the standard 1-MPNN definition used earlier. While context suggests they mean the guarded fragment, explicitly using a different symbol (e.g., \text{G-MPNNs}) or clarifying that they are redefining the shorthand would improve precision.

comments.title: Grammatical phrasing obscures logical flow
comments.quote: The connection between the number of index variables in expressions and _k_ -WL remains to hold.
comments.explanation: The phrase 'remains to hold' is grammatically awkward and likely a typo for 'remains valid' or 'continues to hold'. While easily decipherable in context, it momentarily disrupts the reading of a key transitional sentence summarizing prior results before introducing the summation depth discussion. A minor editorial correction is needed.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper presents a significant theoretical advancement in the field of graph neural networks by introducing a tensor language framework to formally characterize the separation power of GNN architectures. The introduction is exceptionally clear, rigorously defining the motivation—specifically, the difficulty of applying Weisfeiler-Leman (WL) tests to general architectures—and skillfully framing the proposed solution as an abstract "toolbox" that translates structural properties of tensor expressions (summation depth, indices) into graph-theoretic complexity. The writing is precise and flows logically, successfully distinguishing the new contribution from existing work (e.g., Balcilar et al.) while highlighting the novelty of defining higher-order MPNNs. However, a significant issue for a reviewer is the density of the introduction regarding related work in query languages and algebra; the text pivots rapidly between GNN architectures and established concepts in graph algorithms. Care must be taken to ensure that the reader does not become bogged down in the specific definitions of previous languages (like MATLANG) before the core contribution is established, as the novelty of the new tensor language must remain the focal point of the narrative.

comments.title: Non-standard notation for CR and WL update rules
comments.explanation: The authors define the update rules for Color Refinement (CR) and the $k$-dimensional Weisfeiler-Leman ($k$-WL) algorithm using a 'history-keeping' vector notation ($L_{t+1} = (L_t, 	ext{agg})$), rather than the standard folklore definitions which often use synchronous updates or forgetting strategies (e.g., $L_{t+1} = 	ext{hash}(	ext{agg})$). While mathematically valid for reasoning about accumulated information, this notation risks confusion for readers familiar with standard algorithmic definitions (where CR is usually defined as a synchronous coloring process without explicit tuple notation, and $WL$ is defined synchronously but over the full vertex set). The authors later clarify the distinguishing factor (use of non-adjacent vertices), but the tuple notation obscures the fact that both algorithms are implicitly history-preserving in this formulation.

comments.title: Ambiguous LaTeX formatting in WL atomic type description
comments.quote: vector in R[2[(]_[k]_ 2[)][+]_[kℓ] . The first \frac{k(k-1)}{2} entries are 0/1-values...
comments.explanation: The notation describing the dimension of the atomic type vector in the $k$-WL algorithm is garbled by OCR artifacts (`[2[(]_[k]_ 2[)]`), making it unclear whether the dimension is intended to be $2^{ rac{k(k-1)}{2}}$ (hashing) or $ rac{k(k-1)}{2}$ (plain bits), or if it represents something else entirely. The subsequent prose correctly identifies the count of pairs as $ rac{k(k-1)}{2}$, suggesting the LaTeX likely meant this term. While the intent is clear from the text, the formula block introduces ambiguity regarding the actual vector length.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in layer output representation
comments.quote: $[[\psi_s, v]]_G = F_{is'}$ for $\mathbf{F}' = \sigma(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{F} \cdot \mathbf{W})$
comments.explanation: In the provided text, the authors introduce $\psi_s$ as one of the expressions representing the computation of the layer $\mathbf{F}' = \sigma(\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{F} \cdot \mathbf{W})$. The standard notation for the output matrix of this operation is $\mathbf{F}'$. The text incorrectly writes the output value as $F_{is'}$. The notation $F_{is'}$ conflates the input matrix $\mathbf{F}$ with the output $\mathbf{F}'$ and introduces an index $s'$ that is not defined elsewhere in the text (indices typically run from $1$ to $\ell$). While context suggests the authors meant the output value $F'_{is}$, the printed expression $F_{is'}$ is ambiguous and likely a typo for the output matrix notation.

comments.title: Truncated Author Name (Pabl)
comments.quote: Juan Pabl Silva
comments.explanation: The citation lists 'Juan Pabl Silva', which is likely a truncation of the correct name 'Juan Pablo Silva'. The missing 'o' is a clear OCR artifact or typo. While the author name is identifiable in context, the malformed string deviates from standard naming conventions and suggests an indexing issue.

comments.title: Garbled Characters in Author Name (Ga¨uz`ere)
comments.quote: Benoit Ga¨uz`ere
comments.explanation: The string 'Ga¨uz`ere' contains non-standard characters (u`` and `` ` ``) which likely represent the diacritic for 'é' in the name 'Gauzère'. This corruption makes the name difficult to read correctly without applying corrections.

comments.title: Malformed Diacritic in Author Name (Li´o)
comments.quote: Pietro Li´o
comments.explanation: The character sequence 'Li´o' does not conform to standard Unicode for the name 'Lió' (with an acute accent on the o). It likely resulted from an OCR misinterpretation of the accent character.
